title: Defining a Tool on a Server
concept: Declaring a tool's name, description, and input schema so a client and model can understand how to call it.

exercises.prompt: Recapping MCP basics: what is the relationship between an MCP server and an MCP client?

options.text: A server exposes tools, and a client connects to and uses them.
A client hosts the tools, and the server asks permission to run them.
Servers and clients are the exact same thing in the MCP ecosystem.

exercises.explain: In MCP, the server advertises tools, resources, and prompts, which the client applications and LLMs connect to and consume.

cards.title: Giving LLMs Superpowers
cards.body: When you build an LLM application, how does the model know what external actions it can perform? It relies on tools advertised by a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server.

cards.title: Declaring a Server Tool
cards.body: To let an LLM use a tool, your MCP server must declare its name, write a clear description explaining when to use it, and define an input schema outlining the exact variables and types it accepts.

cards.title: The Restaurant Menu Analogy
cards.body: Think of declaring a tool like writing a entry in a restaurant menu. You must list the dish's name, describe what ingredients it contains, and specify how the customer must order it (e.g., choosing a spice level).

cards.title: A Real Tool Declaration
cards.body: To define a 'calculate_tax' tool, you set the name to 'calculate_tax', write a description like 'Calculates sales tax for a given state', and provide a JSON schema requiring a 'price' as a number and 'state' as a string.

cards.title: The LLM Cannot See Your Code
cards.body: You might think the LLM reads your code to figure out how a tool works. In reality, the LLM only sees the metadata (name, description, and schema) you expose; it never reads your actual backend code!

cards.title: Interface vs. Implementation
cards.body: Compare what the LLM sees vs. what the Server actually does behind the scenes when a tool is defined.

left.text: Metadata: Name, human description, and JSON schema of expected inputs.

right.text: Execution: The actual code, database queries, or API calls that produce the result.

exercises.prompt: Why is it crucial to write a detailed, clear description for your MCP server tool?

options.text: To compile the server code faster
To help the LLM understand when and why it should run this specific tool
To generate user-facing documentation websites automatically

exercises.explain: The description is read directly by the LLM (the client model) to determine if this tool is appropriate for the user's current request.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Declaring an input schema helps ensure the LLM sends arguments with the correct names and data types.
exercises.explain: An input schema prevents the LLM from sending messy or incorrectly typed arguments, ensuring your server receives clean data.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to identify the standard format used to describe a tool's inputs:
exercises.sentence: The MCP server defines tool parameters using a structured JSON Schema format.
exercises.explain: A JSON Schema is the standard format used to define the structure, types, and required fields for the tool's input parameters.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tool definitions looks like a hallucinated, non-standard MCP configuration?

options.text: { "name": "magic_tool", "llm_autopilot": true, "parameters": "Just ask for anything and I will guess the input format" }

exercises.explain: Option C is typical of LLM-generated hallucinated code because 'llm_autopilot' is not a standard MCP parameter or schema format, whereas options A and B represent standard structured tool definitions.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a custom weather tool for your MCP server. What are the three essential components you must define so an LLM can successfully locate, understand, and call your tool?

options.text: Only the tool name is required; the LLM automatically guesses the parameters.
Name, description, and an input schema defining the exact parameter types and required fields.
A Python function signature with no description or schemas.
A raw text prompt template without any structured JSON formatting.

exercises.explain: A tool definition must include a clear name, a human-like description for the LLM to read, and a strict JSON Schema detailing the required and optional inputs. Without the schema, the LLM won't know how to format its arguments.
